Martin Strydom

Tuesday March 31 2026, 7.05pm BST, The Times Oil prices recorded their biggest monthly gain in history after the Iran war plunged energy markets into crisis. Brent crude, the global benchmark, rose 63.3 per cent over March, from less than $73 a barrel at the end of February to trade at $118.35 on Tuesday night, up 4.9 per cent on the day, and 94.5 per cent higher since the start of the year. Brent rose as much as 6 per cent at one stage on Tuesday, almost matching the highest prices seen so far during the crisis, after Iran attacked a tanker off the coast of the United Arab Emirates. The monthly gain, in both dollar and percentage terms, tops the 46 per cent increase in September 1990 during the first Gulf war. The US-Israeli attacks on Iran have resulted in Tehran retaliating by effectively shutting down the Strait of Hormuz, a   ...    Advertisement

Prior to the conflict, oil prices had been subdued by predictions of a looming global supply glut. However the loss of shipments through the strait has caused what the International Energy Agency has described as “the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market”.
